answer:
- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham, 1976.** Jobs with autonomy, task significance, skill variety, task identity, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and job satisfaction. This supports prioritizing interesting work when “interesting” means challenging, autonomous, meaningful, and feedback-rich.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation” — Gagné & Deci, 2005.** Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central predictors of sustained motivation and well-being at work. The paper supports valuing intrinsically motivating work, but not “almost exclusively,” because social context and support also matter.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014.** Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance quality, while extrinsic incentives also predict performance, especially quantity. This argues that interesting work matters a lot, but salary, incentives, and role structure are not irrelevant.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.** Tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when experienced as controlling. This is relevant if a higher-paying role makes you trade autonomy and intellectual curiosity for obligation or status.

- **“Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity” — Amabile et al., 1996.** Creativity is higher in environments with challenging work, freedom, resources, supervisory encouragement, and organizational support. For a research-heavy career, the “interestingness” of the problems and the freedom to pursue them may be especially valuable.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.** Person–job and person–organization fit predict job satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. The best choice is likely not “interestingness alone,” but fit between your motives, the role, the team, and the organization.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012.** People perform better when their work aligns with their vocational interests. This supports your friends’ claim that interest is underrated in career choice, especially for long-run excellence.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge et al., 2010.** Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction, though it relates more strongly to satisfaction with pay itself. A somewhat higher salary should probably not dominate the decision unless it materially changes your life or security.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023.** Higher income is associated with greater well-being for many people, but the relationship is roughly logarithmic and varies across individuals. Money matters, but marginal salary increases often matter less than day-to-day work experience, autonomy, and stress.

- **“The Job Demands–Resources Model of Burnout” — Demerouti et al., 2001.** High demands increase burnout risk when not balanced by resources such as autonomy, support, feedback, and recovery. A leadership role could be fulfilling, but if it replaces interesting technical work with demands you dislike, the title alone may not compensate.

- **“Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work” — Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001.** People can reshape tasks, relationships, and interpretations of their jobs to make work more meaningful. A less-interesting role may become better if you can actively craft it toward research, strategy, mentoring, or technical leadership.

- **“Affective Forecasting” — Wilson & Gilbert, 2003.** People often mispredict how future life changes will affect their happiness, especially by overfocusing on salient features and underweighting adaptation and daily routines. For this decision, the key question is not just “salary/team lead/interesting problems,” but what your average Tuesday will feel like.

answer:
**"Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" — Hackman & Oldham (1976)**  
Autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback predict internal motivation and job satisfaction. This supports heavily weighting intellectually engaging work, but it frames “interesting” as part of a broader job-design bundle.

**"Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features" — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)**  
This meta-analysis finds that motivational work characteristics strongly predict satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It also finds that social support, interdependence, workload, and context matter, so “interesting work almost exclusively” is too strong.

**"Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance" — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014)**  
Intrinsic motivation predicts performance especially for complex, high-quality, creative work. Extrinsic incentives also predict performance, so the evidence favors “intrinsic matters a lot,” not “ignore everything else.”

**"A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation" — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999)**  
Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when they feel controlling. This supports caution about optimizing for pay/title, though lab reward effects do not directly answer a whole-career decision.

**"Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation" — Gagné & Deci (2005)**  
Work is more satisfying and sustainable when it supports autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Interesting research likely supports autonomy and competence, but leading a team could also support competence and relatedness if it fits you.

**"Vocational Interests and Performance" — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012)**  
Interest–job fit predicts job performance, training performance, persistence, and lower turnover intentions. This is one of the strongest direct supports for taking “interesting work” seriously in career choice.

**"Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work" — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)**  
Person–job, person–organization, person–group, and person–supervisor fit all predict satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover. The implication is multidimensional: task interest matters, but so do culture, team, manager, and role fit.

**"The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction" — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)**  
Pay level has a positive but relatively modest relationship with job satisfaction. This suggests a somewhat higher salary should matter, but usually less than people expect once baseline needs and fairness concerns are met.

**"Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year" — Killingsworth (2021)**  
Experienced well-being continues to rise with income beyond previously proposed plateaus. This weakens any claim that salary is irrelevant, though it does not imply salary gains dominate daily work quality.

**"High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being" — Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**  
Higher income strongly predicts life evaluation, while day-to-day emotional well-being showed diminishing returns in their data. For already high earners, this supports prioritizing daily experience, but later work shows the income story is more nuanced.

**"Jobs, Careers, and Callings" — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997)**  
People who experience work as a calling report higher satisfaction and fulfillment than those oriented mainly toward jobs or careers. This supports the value of meaningful, absorbing work, but it is correlational rather than a rule to ignore advancement.

**"Outcomes of Meaningful Work" — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay (2019)**  
Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, commitment, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions. It supports optimizing for meaning, but meaningfulness can come from mission, mastery, leadership, or relationships—not only technical interestingness.

**"Motivating Creativity in Organizations" — Amabile (1997)**  
Intrinsic motivation is especially important for creativity and original problem-solving. For research engineering, this supports giving unusually high weight to how curious, absorbed, and self-directed the work makes you feel.

**"Decision and Experience: Why Don’t We Choose What Makes Us Happy?" — Hsee & Hastie (2006)**  
People often choose based on attributes that are easy to compare rather than attributes that drive later experienced well-being. Salary and title are easy to compare, while day-to-day intellectual interest is harder to quantify and may therefore be underweighted.

**"Goal Striving, Need Satisfaction, and Longitudinal Well-Being" — Sheldon & Elliot (1999)**  
People gain more well-being from goals that are self-concordant, meaning aligned with their own interests and values. If leading a team is a genuine long-standing aspiration, the research would count that as intrinsic value too, not merely an external reward.

**"Transformational and Transactional Leadership" — Judge & Piccolo (2004)**  
Leadership quality is strongly related to follower satisfaction, motivation, and performance. A team-lead role may therefore be meaningful and motivating if you value developing people, but it is a different source of satisfaction than doing fascinating research yourself.**"Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want" — Wilson & Gilbert (2005)**  
People systematically mispredict how future situations will affect their happiness. This supports skepticism toward whichever feature feels most salient now, whether that is higher salary, management status, or intellectual excitement.

**Structuring paper titles and summaries**

I'm considering how to format paper titles correctly, including punctuation as part of the name. Each title will need a two-sentence summary. I’ll create a list with titles like: "Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion", and follow with their respective summaries. This applies to others too, such as "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature," along with "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved," and "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory."
answer:
**Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion**  
People tend to overestimate how much higher income will improve day-to-day happiness because money becomes overly salient when they think about it. This supports discounting a modest salary increase relative to daily work experience, but not ignoring money entirely.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
Pay level is only weakly related to job satisfaction, much less than people often assume. This suggests that a somewhat higher salary is unlikely to compensate for substantially less engaging work.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved**  
Income is positively related to emotional well-being for many people, but the relationship is roughly logarithmic and varies across individuals. The paper argues against both “money doesn’t matter” and “money dominates everything.”

**Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
Jobs are more motivating when they provide skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. “Interesting work” matters partly because it often bundles several of these features together.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
Autonomy, task significance, complexity, feedback, and social support are strongly related to satisfaction and motivation. The evidence favors optimizing the whole work design, not focusing exclusively on abstract interestingness.

**Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation**  
Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central predictors of intrinsic motivation and well-being at work. Interesting research may score highly on competence and autonomy, while leading a team may score highly on autonomy and relatedness.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting, especially when rewards feel controlling. This cautions against letting salary or status displace intrinsically motivating work, but context and reward framing matter.

**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research**  
People perform better and persist longer when their work fits their interests. This supports taking “interestingness” seriously as a predictor of both success and satisfaction.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit**  
Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts job satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. The relevant question is broader than whether the work is interesting: it is whether the whole environment fits you.

**Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis**  
Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal. This supports prioritizing work that feels important, but most evidence is correlational rather than definitive causal proof.

**The Job Demands–Resources Model of Burnout**  
Burnout is predicted by high demands and insufficient resources such as autonomy, support, and feedback. Highly interesting work can still become bad if workload, pressure, or organizational constraints overwhelm the resources.

**Do People Choose What Makes Them Happy? Theory and Evidence from Subjective Well-Being**  
People’s choices track predicted happiness, but they also trade off happiness against purpose, control, family, status, morality, and other values. This argues against the claim that research supports optimizing almost exclusively for interestingness.

**Analyzing motivation and performance**

answer:
- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature”** — This meta-analysis finds that motivational job features—autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and complexity—are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction, internal motivation, and performance. It supports putting substantial weight on the day-to-day interestingness/design of the work, while also showing that social context and working conditions matter.

- **“The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis”** — Core job characteristics in the Job Characteristics Model are consistently related to satisfaction, motivation, absenteeism, and performance, though effect sizes vary. This is classic evidence that enriched, meaningful, autonomous work is a major driver of outcomes, but not evidence for ignoring other factors.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature”** — Across studies, pay level shows a weak relationship with overall job satisfaction and a stronger but still limited relationship with pay satisfaction. For already well-paid jobs, the marginal salary increase is often less predictive of satisfaction than differences in job content and fit.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis”** — Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially performance quality, while extrinsic incentives more strongly predict performance quantity. The paper argues against an “almost exclusively” rule: intrinsic interest matters a lot, but incentives and role structure can add independent value.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation”** — This meta-analysis finds that expected, tangible, performance-contingent rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting. It is relevant when a higher-paying offer pulls you away from work you find intrinsically engaging, though the evidence is experimental rather than a direct career-choice study.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation”** — The review argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central psychological needs at work, and that motivation is highest when goals are autonomous or well-internalized. A management role can be motivating if it satisfies these needs, so “interesting work” may include leadership, mentoring, and ownership rather than only technical novelty.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit”** — Person–job, person–organization, person–group, and person–supervisor fit all predict job satisfaction, commitment, and lower turnover intentions. This suggests evaluating the whole role fit—research topic, leadership duties, culture, manager, values—not just salary or abstract prestige.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research”** — Vocational interest congruence predicts job performance, training performance, and persistence, with stronger effects when interests map closely onto job tasks. It supports treating genuine interest as a career-performance input, not merely a preference to indulge after more “objective” factors.

- **“Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis”** — Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions, with smaller but positive links to performance. The paper supports giving major weight to meaning and purpose, while noting that much of the evidence is correlational.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work”** — People who experience their work as a calling report higher satisfaction and stronger attachment to work, and such orientations appear across many occupations. It suggests that whether work feels like a calling can dominate its experienced quality, but also that people differ in preferring a job, career, or calling orientation.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion”** — This paper argues that people overestimate the effect of income on happiness because thinking about money creates a focusing illusion. It supports the claim that salary can be overweighted in decisions relative to daily activities, relationships, autonomy, and experienced work content.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved”** — This reconciliation finds that experienced well-being generally rises with income, but the pattern is heterogeneous and roughly logarithmic, with a plateau mainly among the least happy group. It is a useful counterweight: salary is not irrelevant, but at high income levels its marginal effect may be smaller than large differences in work quality.

- **“Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead”** — Motivation to lead is a distinct individual difference that predicts leadership experiences, leadership training success, and leadership potential beyond some standard traits. If leading a small team is a long-standing intrinsic goal, the Meta role’s management component is itself a potentially important source of fit and motivation.

answer:
- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham, 1976**  
  Jobs are more motivating when they provide skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. “Interesting work” matters, but the paper frames it as one part of broader job design rather than the only variable.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**  
  Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. This suggests the choice should include intellectual interest, culture, manager/team fit, and desired role identity.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014**  
  Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance, especially for complex and creative work. Extrinsic incentives also matter, so the evidence supports weighting interest heavily but not almost exclusively.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999**  
  Expected, contingent tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. This supports skepticism toward choosing mainly for pay or status when the work itself becomes less compelling.

- **“Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity” — Amabile et al., 1996**  
  Creativity is higher in environments with autonomy, encouragement, sufficient resources, challenging work, and low organizational impediments. For research engineering, the day-to-day creative climate may matter as much as the nominal topic or compensation.

- **“Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis” — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay, 2019**  
  Meaningful work is associated with higher job satisfaction, engagement, organizational commitment, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions. The findings support prioritizing meaning and interest, but they do not show these should dominate all other considerations.

- **“The Path Taken: Consequences of Attaining Intrinsic and Extrinsic Aspirations in Post-College Life” — Niemiec, Ryan & Deci, 2009**  
  Attaining intrinsic goals such as personal growth, relationships, and community contribution predicted greater well-being. Attaining extrinsic goals such as money, fame, and image did not produce comparable well-being gains and was sometimes linked to ill-being.

- **“A Dark Side of the American Dream: Correlates of Financial Success as a Central Life Aspiration” — Kasser & Ryan, 1993**  
  People who place unusually high importance on financial success tend to report lower well-being and more distress. This supports not over-weighting salary increases when core work motivation may drop.

- **“High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being” — Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**  
  Higher income was associated with better life evaluation, while emotional well-being showed diminishing gains beyond moderate income levels in their data. For already well-paid professionals, marginal salary increases may matter less than daily work experience.

- **“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth, 2021**  
  This paper challenged the strong income-plateau claim, finding experienced well-being continues to rise with income for many people. It weakens any simplistic claim that salary barely matters, though the relationship remains roughly logarithmic and therefore marginal gains shrink.

- **“What Do You Think Would Make You Happier? What Do You Think You Would Choose?” — Benjamin, Heffetz, Kimball & Rees-Jones, 2012**  
  People’s choices are strongly related to predicted happiness but also reflect other values such as purpose, control, family, status, and morality. This suggests “choose the most interesting thing” is too narrow if leadership, growth, or impact are independently valued.

- **“Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead” — Chan & Drasgow, 2001**  
  Motivation to lead varies across people and predicts leadership-related behaviors and development. If leading a team is genuinely intrinsically motivating for you, it may count as “interesting work” in a broader sense rather than merely an external perk.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997**  
  People differ in whether they experience work primarily as a job, career, or calling, and these orientations relate to satisfaction and meaning. The same role can feel very different depending on whether it advances intellectual curiosity, leadership identity, mission, or status.

- **“Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want” — Wilson & Gilbert, 2005**  
  People often mispredict the intensity and duration of future emotions from life changes. This cautions against overconfidence about how much a higher salary, team-lead title, or more interesting research agenda will affect future well-being.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014.** Intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially quality and complex/creative work, while extrinsic incentives also predict performance, especially quantity. - **“Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis” — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay, 2019.** Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions. This supports prioritizing meaning and interest, but meaning can come from impact, leadership, mastery, or mission—not only research novelty.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010.** Pay is positively but only modestly related to job satisfaction. For already well-paid roles, a somewhat higher salary may be less important than a large difference in intrinsic fit.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023.** Well-being generally rises with income, though the gains are often modest and differ across people. This argues against ignoring salary, but also against assuming a salary bump will compensate for a substantially worse daily experience.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.** Expected tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting, especially when rewards feel controlling. This is relevant when a role’s appeal shifts from the work itself to compensation, title, or status.

- **“The ‘What’ and ‘Why’ of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior” — Deci & Ryan, 2000.** Self-determination theory argues that sustained motivation and well-being depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Interesting research may satisfy competence and autonomy, while leading a team may satisfy autonomy, relatedness, and impact.

- **“Goal Striving, Need Satisfaction, and Longitudinal Well-Being: The Self-Concordance Model” — Sheldon & Elliot, 1999.** Goals aligned with one’s deeper interests and values lead to more sustained effort, greater attainment, and higher well-being. This supports choosing the path that feels self-concordant rather than the one that merely looks better externally.

- **“Linking Job Demands and Resources to Employee Engagement and Burnout: A Theoretical Extension and Meta-Analytic Test” — Crawford, LePine & Rich, 2010.** Job resources are associated with engagement, while hindrance demands are associated with burnout. Leading a team may add resources like autonomy and influence, but also demands like coordination, conflict, and administrative load.

answer:
**“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007).** Work design features such as autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and social support strongly predict job satisfaction, motivation, strain, and performance. This supports heavily weighting the day-to-day substance of work, but not reducing the decision to “interestingness” alone.

**“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010).** Pay level is only modestly related to job satisfaction and pay satisfaction across studies. This supports not overweighting a somewhat higher salary, while still not implying salary is irrelevant.

**“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2006).** People tend to overestimate the effect of income on day-to-day happiness because income becomes salient when evaluating life choices. This is evidence that compensation can be overweighted relative to daily experience.

**“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023).** Experienced well-being generally rises with income, though with diminishing and heterogeneous effects. This tempers the claim that money barely matters, especially when salary affects security, freedom, or future optionality.

**“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Ryan & Deci (2000).** Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central psychological needs behind motivation and well-being. Interesting research may satisfy competence and autonomy, while leading a team may satisfy autonomy and relatedness.

**“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999).** Tangible extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks under some conditions. This supports protecting intrinsic interest, but it does not support ignoring extrinsic rewards altogether.

**“On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives” — Heath (1999).** People systematically overestimate how much others are motivated by money and external incentives, and underestimate intrinsic motivators. This is direct evidence for an “intrinsic motivation is underrated” bias, though mainly in judgments about others.

**“Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis” — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay (2019).** Meaningful work is strongly associated with engagement, job satisfaction, organizational commitment, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal. This supports prioritizing meaningful work, but meaning can come from impact, mentorship, responsibility, and identity—not only technical novelty.

**“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005).** Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and retention. This implies “interesting work” is one form of fit, but culture, team, manager, and values fit also matter.

**“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012).** Vocational interests predict performance, especially when people’s interests match their work environments. This supports treating interest as a real career-relevant variable, not merely a perk.

**“Motivation and Creativity: Effects of Motivational Orientation on Creative Writers” — Amabile (1985).** Intrinsic motivation was associated with more creative output than extrinsic motivational orientation. For research and creative engineering work, this supports the idea that interesting, self-motivating work can improve both satisfaction and quality of output.

**“The Job Demands–Resources Model: State of the Art” — Bakker & Demerouti (2007).** Engagement and burnout depend on both job resources, such as autonomy and support, and job demands, such as workload and emotional strain. A team-lead role could add resources and career growth while also adding managerial demands.

**“The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work” — Bunderson & Thompson (2009).** People with a strong calling experienced deep meaning but were also more willing to accept sacrifice, low pay, and difficult conditions. This cautions against focusing on meaningful or interesting work “almost exclusively.”

**“Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?” — O’Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018).** Believing interests are fixed can reduce openness to new domains and persistence when challenges arise. This suggests that a currently less-interesting leadership role might become more interesting as skills, identity, and mastery develop.

- **Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved** (Kahneman, Killingsworth, & Mellers, 2023) — Emotional well-being generally rises with income on a logarithmic scale, but gains are smaller at higher income levels and vary across people. This argues against saying salary is irrelevant, while also suggesting that marginal salary increases may not dominate daily work experience.

- **Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** (Ryan & Deci, 2000) — Well-being and motivation are supported by autonomy, competence, and relatedness. - **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** (Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson, 2005) — Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. This suggests comparing the roles across multiple dimensions rather than reducing the decision to interestingness alone.

- **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work** (Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz, 1997) — People differ in whether they see work mainly as a job, career, or calling, and these orientations shape satisfaction and meaning. - **Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?** (O’Keefe, Dweck, & Walton, 2018) — People who believe interests are fixed are more likely to avoid unfamiliar domains and expect passion to be effortless. This cautions against treating “less interesting now” as permanent, especially for a role involving a desired new skill like leadership.

- **Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want** (Wilson & Gilbert, 2005) — People often mispredict how future situations will affect their happiness because they overfocus on salient differences and underweight adaptation. This is relevant because “more interesting research,” “higher salary,” and “leading a team” may all feel different in imagination than in daily reality.

answer:
**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
Jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback tend to produce higher motivation, satisfaction, and performance. “Interesting work” matters, but a team-lead role could also score highly if it adds autonomy, impact, and feedback.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit**  
Fit between a person and their job or organization predicts job satisfaction, commitment, lower turnover intentions, and performance. This supports taking intellectual fit seriously rather than optimizing mainly for salary or title.

**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research**  
People perform better and persist longer when their work matches their interests. The evidence supports “doing interesting things” as practically important, not merely a luxury preference.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being**  
Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are core drivers of motivation and well-being. The paper supports prioritizing intrinsically motivating work, but not ignoring external rewards or career goals when they serve those needs.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis**  
Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives both predict performance, with intrinsic motivation especially important for quality and complex work. This argues against focusing on “interestingness” almost exclusively, while still making it central for research-like work.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
Tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation when they feel controlling, especially for already-interesting tasks. Higher pay alone may not compensate for losing intrinsically engaging work, depending on how the new role changes autonomy and purpose.

**Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do**  
Intrinsic motivation is a major component of creativity, particularly when people have autonomy and feel challenged. For research engineering, a less interesting role may reduce creative output unless the leadership component becomes intrinsically engaging.

**Optimal Experience in Work and Leisure**  
People often experience flow more at work than in leisure when tasks are challenging and skill-matched. This supports the idea that absorbing, interesting work can be underrated relative to more obvious rewards.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
Pay is positively but only modestly related to job satisfaction and pay satisfaction. This suggests that a somewhat higher salary may have limited effect if the work itself is meaningfully less engaging.

**High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being**  
Higher income improves life evaluation, but emotional well-being showed diminishing returns after a threshold in this study. For already well-paid workers, day-to-day experience of the work may matter more than incremental compensation.

**Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year**  
This paper challenges the income-plateau finding and finds experienced well-being continues to rise with income, roughly logarithmically. Salary should not be dismissed, but the marginal gains may still be smaller than changes in daily work experience.

**Job, Career, and Calling: People’s Relations to Their Work**  
People relate to work as a job, career, or calling, and those with a calling orientation report higher satisfaction and work centrality. The paper implies that “interesting and meaningful” work can be a major source of well-being, but career advancement is also a distinct legitimate value.

**The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work**  
Deeply meaningful work can produce strong identity and satisfaction, but also willingness to accept lower pay, worse conditions, or exploitation. This cautions that interesting work is valuable, but “almost exclusively” prioritizing it can have costs.
prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in OpenAI doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from Meta - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

answer:
- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham, 1976.**  
  Jobs with autonomy, skill variety, task identity, task significance, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and satisfaction. This supports prioritizing genuinely engaging work, but “interesting” is only one component of good job design.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007.**  
  A large meta-analysis finds that motivational job features, especially autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback, strongly predict job satisfaction and motivation. It suggests day-to-day work content is often more important than external job attributes like title or pay.

- **“Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis” — Hoff, Song, Wee, Phan & Rounds, 2020.**  
  People are more satisfied when their work matches their interests, though the effect is meaningful rather than overwhelming. This supports your friends’ point, but not the claim that interest should dominate almost exclusively.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012.**  
  Interest fit predicts job performance, training performance, and persistence across many occupations. Interesting work is not just pleasant; it can also compound into better long-term achievement.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.**  
  Person–job and person–organization fit are strongly related to satisfaction, commitment, and lower turnover intentions. This argues for evaluating both the intellectual work and the surrounding environment/team, not only compensation or role level.

- **“Self-Concordance, Goal Attainment, and the Pursuit of Happiness: Can There Be an Upward Spiral?” — Sheldon & Houser-Marko, 2001.**  
  Goals aligned with personal interests and values lead to greater effort, achievement, and well-being. This supports choosing work that feels internally compelling rather than mainly externally rewarded.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997.**  
  People who experience their work as a calling report greater fulfillment than those who view it mainly as a job or career ladder. The paper suggests meaning and intrinsic engagement can matter more than status, but it also shows people frame similar work differently.

- **“The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions” — Grant, 2008.**  
  Seeing how one’s work benefits others increases motivation, persistence, and performance. For AI research roles, perceived impact and mission may be central sources of motivation beyond abstract “interestingness.”

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.**  
  Controlling extrinsic rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. This cautions against letting pay or title crowd out work you find intrinsically absorbing.

- **“High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being” — Kahneman & Deaton, 2010.**  
  Higher income improves life evaluation, but its relationship with day-to-day emotional well-being shows diminishing returns. This weakens the case for choosing a somewhat higher salary if the daily work is much less engaging.

- **“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth, 2021.**  
  Contrary to a simple plateau story, experienced well-being continues to rise with income for many people. This means salary is not irrelevant, but the effects are usually gradual rather than transformative.

- **“Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction” — Schkade & Kahneman, 1998.**  
  People often overweight salient differences when predicting happiness and underweight daily lived experience. In job choice, this suggests titles, prestige, and salary can loom larger than the actual texture of everyday work.

- **“Promotions and the Peter Principle” — Benson, Li & Shue, 2019.**  
  Strong individual contributors are often promoted into management, but prior individual performance does not necessarily predict managerial effectiveness. This is relevant because “leading a small team” may be fulfilling, but it is a different job with different sources of satisfaction and stress.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton, 2015.**  
  Boss quality has large effects on worker productivity, showing that management can be highly consequential. If leading is a genuine long-term aspiration, the opportunity has real career value, but the paper does not imply management is personally more satisfying than deep technical work.

answer:
**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** — Hackman & Oldham (1976)  
This paper finds that autonomy, feedback, skill variety, task identity, and task significance predict intrinsic motivation and job satisfaction. It supports prioritizing engaging work, but treats “interesting” as one part of a broader work-design package.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)  
This meta-analysis shows that autonomy, task significance, feedback, social support, interdependence, and work context all predict satisfaction, motivation, performance, and stress. It argues against focusing almost exclusively on intellectual interest, because team structure and daily work conditions matter too.

**Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation** — Gagné & Deci (2005)  
This review argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central drivers of work motivation and well-being. Interesting research may satisfy these needs, but leading a team could also satisfy them if it provides growth, agency, and meaningful relationships.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis** — Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014)  
This meta-analysis finds that intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially quality of performance. It also finds that incentives can matter, so the evidence supports weighting interesting work heavily but not exclusively.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999)  
This meta-analysis finds that some controlling, contingent rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting. It supports caution about choosing mainly for pay, especially when the current work is already highly motivating.

**Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover** — Van Iddekinge, Roth, Putka, & Lanivich (2011)  
This meta-analysis finds that vocational interests predict job performance and turnover, particularly when interests are well matched to job content. It supports taking “interestingness” seriously, but the effects are not so dominant that other job attributes become irrelevant.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)  
This meta-analysis finds that fit with the job, organization, group, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, turnover intentions, and performance. It suggests comparing OpenAI and Meta across multiple forms of fit, not just research topic interest.

**Self-Concordance, Goal Attainment, and the Pursuit of Happiness: Can There Be an Upward Spiral?** — Sheldon & Elliot (1999)  
This paper finds that goals aligned with personal interests and values are more likely to be achieved and to increase well-being when achieved. It is relevant because “leading a small team” may be self-concordant even if the technical work is less interesting.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)  
This meta-analysis finds that pay level is only weakly related to overall job satisfaction. It supports not over-weighting a modest salary increase when both options are already well paid.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved** — Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers (2023)  
This paper finds that higher income is associated with higher experienced well-being for most people, though the relationship is not unlimited or uniform. It implies salary should count, but probably should not dominate a decision between two high-compensation research roles.

**Job Satisfaction and Subjective Well-Being: A Meta-Analysis** — Bowling, Eschleman, & Wang (2010)  
This meta-analysis finds a substantial association between job satisfaction and broader subjective well-being. It supports caring a lot about the role that will make your day-to-day work life satisfying.

**Differential Challenge Stressor–Hindrance Stressor Relationships with Job Attitudes, Turnover Intentions, Turnover, and Withdrawal Behavior: A Meta-Analysis** — Podsakoff, LePine, & LePine (2007)  
This meta-analysis distinguishes challenge stressors, which can relate to motivation and achievement, from hindrance stressors, which are consistently damaging. A team-lead role could be beneficial if it creates valued challenge, but harmful if it mainly adds politics, ambiguity, or bureaucracy.

**Boss Competence and Worker Well-Being** — Artz, Goodall, & Oswald (2017)  
This paper finds that employees report higher job satisfaction when their supervisor is technically competent. It makes manager quality and the quality of people you would report to or manage highly relevant to the choice.

**Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction** — Schkade & Kahneman (1998)  
This paper shows that people overemphasize salient differences when predicting future life satisfaction. In a job decision, either “more interesting research” or “higher salary plus leadership” can become the over-focused attribute.

answer:
- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976).** Jobs with skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produced higher internal motivation and satisfaction. This supports prioritizing intrinsically engaging work, but treats “interesting” as one part of broader job design rather than the only factor.

- **“The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis” — Fried & Ferris (1987).** Meta-analysis found that motivating job characteristics reliably relate to satisfaction, motivation, and performance. The evidence favors engaging and autonomous work, but the effects are moderate and context-dependent.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang, and Morgeson (2007).** Motivational features such as autonomy, task significance, and feedback are strong predictors of satisfaction and commitment. Social support, interdependence, and work context also matter, so “interesting work” should not be evaluated in isolation.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation” — Gagné & Deci (2005).** Work is more motivating when it satisfies autonomy, competence, and relatedness. A team-lead role could be better if it increases these needs, but worse if it replaces intrinsically motivating research with coordination or politics.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner, and Ryan (1999).** Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting, especially when they feel controlling. This supports skepticism toward choosing mainly for salary or title, while not implying that compensation is irrelevant.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, and Rich (2010).** Pay level has only a small association with overall job satisfaction. This suggests a modest salary increase is often less important than role content, autonomy, and fit once financial needs are comfortably met.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz, and Stone (2006).** People tend to overestimate the happiness impact of income when attention is focused on income. This supports the idea that salary can be overweighted relative to daily lived work experience.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman, and Mellers (2023).** Emotional well-being generally rises with income, though the pattern differs across people and plateaus mainly for a less-happy subgroup. This weakens any claim that money should be ignored, but also does not make it a substitute for good day-to-day work.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, and Johnson (2005).** Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. This argues against focusing almost exclusively on interesting tasks, because culture, people, values, and manager relationship matter too.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds, and Drasgow (2012).** Alignment between vocational interests and work predicts job and training performance. This supports choosing work aligned with genuine intellectual interests if long-term excellence is a major goal.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, and Schwartz (1997).** People who view work as a calling report higher work and life satisfaction than those who view it mainly as a job or career. This supports meaning and intrinsic connection, but also shows that work orientation varies within the same occupation.

- **“Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work” — Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001).** Employees can reshape tasks, relationships, and interpretations to make work more meaningful. This suggests a less-interesting role might become more interesting through leadership and job crafting, while a currently fascinating role can also evolve.

- **“Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?” — O’Keefe, Dweck, and Walton (2018).** A fixed “find your passion” mindset can narrow exploration and reduce persistence when interests become difficult. This challenges the advice to focus almost exclusively on what is already interesting, favoring a view where interests can be developed.

- **“Understanding Contemporary Forms of Exploitation: Attributions of Passion Serve to Legitimize the Poor Treatment of Workers” — Kim, Campbell, Shepherd, and Kay (2020).** People are more willing to justify lower pay, extra work, and unfair treatment when workers are seen as passionate. This warns that “do the most interesting thing” can be exploited and should be balanced against compensation, authority, and boundaries.

- **“Job Demands, Job Decision Latitude, and Mental Strain: Implications for Job Redesign” — Karasek (1979).** Psychological strain is highest when job demands are high and decision latitude is low. A leadership role may improve well-being if it gives real control, but may hurt if it mainly adds demands without autonomy.

- **“The Good Life of the Powerful: The Experience of Power and Authenticity Enhances Subjective Well-Being” — Kifer, Heller, Perunovic, and Galinsky (2013).** Power is associated with greater subjective well-being partly because it lets people act more authentically. This makes leading a team relevant in its own right if it offers genuine agency aligned with your values.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007.**  
  This meta-analysis finds that motivational job features, especially autonomy, task significance, skill variety, and feedback, are strongly related to satisfaction and engagement. It also finds that social and contextual factors matter, so interesting work is very important but not the only thing.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation” — Gagné & Deci, 2005.**  
  The paper argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are core psychological needs that support sustained motivation and well-being at work. A role can be motivating either through intellectual interest or through leadership, growth, autonomy, and strong relationships.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.**  
  Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting, especially when the rewards feel controlling. This supports caution about optimizing mainly for salary or title, while not implying that compensation is irrelevant.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014.**  
  Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives both predict performance, with intrinsic motivation especially important for quality and persistence. The evidence supports taking interest seriously, but not focusing on it almost exclusively.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012.**  
  Interest fit between a person and their work predicts job performance and training performance. This suggests that doing work you find genuinely interesting can be instrumentally valuable, not just emotionally pleasant.

- **“Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis” — Hoff, Song, Wee, Phan & Rounds, 2020.**  
  The paper finds that fit between vocational interests and work environments is reliably associated with job satisfaction. The effect is meaningful but not totalizing, so interest should be heavily weighted alongside other job attributes.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997.**  
  People who view their work as a calling tend to report higher job and life satisfaction than those who view it mainly as a job or career. The paper supports the importance of meaning and intrinsic engagement, but also shows that people differ in how they relate to work.

- **“The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work” — Bunderson & Thompson, 2009.**  
  Workers with a strong calling derive deep meaning and identity from their jobs. They also accept more sacrifice and lower material rewards, showing that meaningful work can be both valuable and risky.

- **“On the Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review” — Rosso, Dekas & Wrzesniewski, 2010.**  
  This review identifies major sources of work meaning, including self-expression, competence, purpose, belonging, and impact on others. It implies that “interesting work” is only one route to meaning, with leadership and team impact potentially mattering too.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.**  
  Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions. The relevant question is not only which work is more interesting, but which role better fits your needs, values, abilities, and desired identity.

- **“The Job Satisfaction–Job Performance Relationship: A Qualitative and Quantitative Review” — Judge, Thoresen, Bono & Patton, 2001.**  
  This meta-analysis finds a positive relationship between job satisfaction and job performance. Enjoying and valuing the work may therefore affect both well-being and effectiveness.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Examination of the Relationship Between Job Satisfaction and Subjective Well-Being” — Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010.**  
  Job satisfaction and overall subjective well-being are strongly related. Since work occupies much of adult life, differences in day-to-day work experience can matter substantially for happiness.

- **“The Job Demands–Resources Model of Burnout” — Demerouti, Bakker, Nachreiner & Schaufeli, 2001.**  
  Burnout is predicted by high job demands and insufficient resources, not merely by lack of interest. Even interesting jobs can become bad if demands are excessive, and less intrinsically interesting jobs can be good if they provide autonomy, support, and growth.

- **“Valuing Alternative Work Arrangements” — Mas & Pallais, 2017.**  
  This field experiment estimates how much pay workers are willing to trade for non-wage job attributes such as flexibility and scheduling control. It shows that job attributes beyond salary can have large subjective value.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone, 2006.**  
  The paper argues that people often overestimate the effect of income on experienced happiness because income is highly salient when making comparisons. This supports the possibility that salary differences are overweighted relative to daily work content.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023.**  
  The paper reconciles prior findings by showing that experienced well-being generally rises with income, though patterns differ across people. Salary matters, but the gains are not a reason to ignore work quality, meaning, autonomy, or fit.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton, 2015.**  
  The paper finds that supervisors have large effects on worker productivity. This makes team leadership and management quality relevant job attributes rather than secondary details.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014.**

- **“High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being” — Kahneman & Deaton, 2010.**  
  Higher income is associated with better life evaluation, but its relationship with day-to-day emotional well-being is weaker and was found to plateau in their data. This supports discounting salary somewhat when the alternative is substantially more interesting work.

- **“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth, 2021.**  
  This paper challenges the strong income-plateau result and finds that experienced well-being continues to rise with income. The effect exists but is not enormous, so it weakens any simplistic claim that extra salary is psychologically irrelevant.

- **“Self-Concordance, Goal Attainment, and the Pursuit of Happiness: Can There Be an Upward Spiral?” — Sheldon & Elliot, 1999.**  
  Goals aligned with one’s interests and values are pursued with more sustained effort and yield greater well-being when attained. This supports choosing roles that fit your authentic motivations, whether that is frontier research, leadership, or some mix.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997.**  
  People who experience their work as a calling report higher satisfaction and often invest more deeply in it. The paper supports the value of meaningful, interesting work, but also shows people differ in whether they relate to work as a job, career, or calling.

- **“Les Passions de l’Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion” — Vallerand et al., 2003.**  
  Harmonious passion for an activity is associated with well-being, sustained engagement, and healthier functioning. Obsessive passion can produce conflict and burnout, so “do the interesting thing almost exclusively” can be harmful if it crowds out other values.

- **“Promotions and the Peter Principle” — Benson, Li & Shue, 2019.**  
  Firms often promote strong individual contributors into management, but the skills predicting individual performance do not always predict managerial performance. This is relevant because leading a team may satisfy a long-standing goal, but it is a different job from doing research.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton, 2015.**  
  Bosses have large effects on worker productivity, and good managers raise team output substantially. This suggests leadership can be highly impactful and intrinsically rewarding, even if the object-level technical work is less interesting.

- **“Linking Job Demands and Resources to Employee Engagement and Burnout: A Theoretical Extension and Meta-Analytic Test”** — Challenging demands can increase engagement, while hindrance demands tend to increase burnout. - **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being”** — Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central drivers of intrinsic motivation and well-being. - **“Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead”** — Motivation to lead is a distinct and meaningful individual difference that predicts leadership potential and interest. If leading a small team is intrinsically important to you, it should count as part of the “interesting work” side of the ledger, not merely as status.

answer:
- **“Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover” — Van Iddekinge et al., 2011**  
  Vocational interests and interest–job fit predict performance and turnover-related outcomes, though effects are usually modest. This supports treating “will I find the work interesting?” as a serious career variable, not a luxury.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown et al., 2005**  
  Person–job fit is strongly related to job satisfaction, commitment, intent to quit, and performance. The evidence supports prioritizing fit, but fit includes more than interesting tasks: culture, team, manager, and values matter too.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey et al., 2007**  
  Autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and social support predict motivation, satisfaction, performance, stress, and turnover. Interesting work matters, but the broader design of the role matters at least as much.

- **“Self-Determination Theory in Work Organizations: The State of a Science” — Deci, Olafsen, and Ryan, 2017**  
  Autonomy, competence, and relatedness support autonomous motivation, which is associated with better well-being, persistence, and performance. Research work may satisfy autonomy and competence, while team leadership may satisfy relatedness and impact if the role is well designed.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin, and Ford, 2014**  
  Intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially quality and complex-task performance, while extrinsic incentives also predict performance. This supports taking interestingness very seriously, but not focusing on it “almost exclusively.”

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner, and Ryan, 1999**  
  Expected tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when experienced as controlling. A higher salary or title is not automatically harmful, but external rewards can crowd out curiosity-driven motivation if the new role feels less autonomous.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge et al., 2010**  
  Pay level is only weakly to moderately associated with job satisfaction. This supports not overweighting a salary bump if the day-to-day work becomes much less engaging.

- **“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth, 2021**  
  Experience-sampling data show well-being continues to rise with income, roughly logarithmically, beyond earlier proposed satiation points. This is a counterweight: money should not dominate the decision, but it is not irrelevant.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman, and Mellers, 2023**  
  Reanalysis suggests income keeps improving well-being for most people, with a plateau mainly among the least happy subgroup. The implication is that salary matters, but with diminishing returns and large individual variation.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman et al., 2006**  
  People often overestimate income’s effect on day-to-day happiness because income becomes salient during comparison. For career decisions, this warns against overweighting salary and title relative to daily work experience.

- **“Immune Neglect: A Source of Durability Bias in Affective Forecasting” — Gilbert et al., 1998**  
  People overestimate how long future emotional reactions will last because they underestimate adaptation and coping. The thrill of a raise, title, or promotion may fade faster than expected, making recurring work content especially important.

- **“Predictors of Objective and Subjective Career Success: A Meta-Analysis” — Ng et al., 2005**  
  Objective career success and subjective career satisfaction have overlapping but distinct predictors, including human capital, organizational support, sponsorship, and personality. A team-lead role may be valuable if it builds managerial capital you genuinely want, even if it is less intellectually interesting.

- **“The Dynamic Componential Model of Creativity and Innovation in Organizations: Making Progress, Making Meaning” — Amabile and Pratt, 2016**  
  Creativity and innovation depend heavily on intrinsic motivation, meaningful progress, domain skill, and supportive environments. For research engineering, losing genuine interest in the problem can plausibly reduce creativity and long-run excellence.

- **“On the Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review” — Rosso, Dekas, and Wrzesniewski, 2010**  
  Meaningful work comes from multiple sources, including self-realization, contribution, belonging, values, and impact. Interesting technical problems are one source of meaning, but mentoring and leading a team can also be meaningful.

- **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** (Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson, 2005): Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. - **Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** (Ryan & Deci, 2000): Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are core psychological needs that support motivation and well-being. - **Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis** (Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford, 2014): Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives both predict performance, with intrinsic motivation especially important for quality and persistence. This argues against focusing “almost exclusively” on either interesting work or compensation.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** (Deci, Koestner, & Ryan, 1999): Controlling extrinsic rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation, while informational or competence-affirming rewards are less harmful. This cautions against chasing compensation when it changes the psychological meaning of the work.

- **Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis** (Allan et al., 2019): Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions. If your current research feels meaningful rather than merely fun, giving that up could be costly.

- **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work** (Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz, 1997): People who see their work as a calling report higher satisfaction and stronger identification with their work. This suggests rare mission-fit can be worth protecting, though some people can craft a calling in a new role.

- **A Meta-Analytic Examination of the Relationship Between Job Satisfaction and Subjective Well-Being** (Bowling, Eschleman, & Wang, 2010): Job satisfaction and overall subjective well-being are substantially related. Since work occupies much of waking life, sustained loss of interest or meaning can spill over into general happiness.

- **The Relationship Between Job Satisfaction and Health: A Meta-Analysis** (Faragher, Cass, & Cooper, 2005): Job dissatisfaction is associated with worse mental and physical health, especially burnout, anxiety, and depression-related outcomes. A less interesting role is risky if it would become chronically dissatisfying rather than merely less exciting.

- **High Income Improves Evaluation of Life But Not Emotional Well-Being** (Kahneman & Deaton, 2010): Higher income is linked to better life evaluation, while day-to-day emotional well-being showed diminishing returns in their data. This supports the idea that, beyond a high baseline, more salary may add less than better daily work experience.

- **Experienced Well-Being Rises With Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year** (Killingsworth, 2021): Experience-sampling data found that experienced well-being continues to rise with income rather than sharply plateauing. This weakens the claim that money stops mattering, but it does not show that money outweighs large differences in work content.

- **Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved** (Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers, 2023): This reanalysis finds that well-being generally rises with income, while a plateau appears mainly for the least happy subgroup. The implication is nuanced: higher pay can matter, but it may not compensate for a role that makes you persistently unhappy.

- **Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction** (Schkade & Kahneman, 1998): People often overweight salient differences when predicting future happiness. In job choice, salary, prestige, or manager title may be over-focal, while daily tasks, colleagues, and autonomy may drive actual experience.

- **Linking Job Demands and Resources to Employee Engagement and Burnout: A Theoretical Extension and Meta-Analytic Test** (Crawford, LePine, & Rich, 2010): Challenge demands can increase engagement, while hindrance demands tend to reduce engagement and increase burnout. Leading a small team may be energizing if it brings autonomy and growth, but draining if it mainly adds bureaucracy and coordination load.
